items.q: How did Hitler come to power in 1933?
items.correct: He was legally appointed Chancellor by President Hindenburg as part of a conservative political deal, after the Nazis became the largest party in the Reichstag.
items.misconception: Hitler seized power through a violent coup or revolution.
items.random_wrong: Hitler won an outright majority in the 1932 presidential election.
items.why: The 1923 Beer Hall Putsch failed; in 1933 Hitler came to power constitutionally. Students confuse Nazi violence with the actual mechanism of takeover, which was legal manoeuvring.

items.q: What was the political significance of the Reichstag Fire?
items.correct: Hitler used it to issue the Reichstag Fire Decree suspending civil liberties, jail Communists, and create the conditions for the Enabling Act of March 1933.
items.misconception: Hitler personally set the fire and blamed it on the Communists.
items.random_wrong: The fire destroyed all of Berlin and forced people to support the Nazis.
items.why: Historians still debate who started it; van der Lubbe likely acted alone. What matters is how Hitler exploited it — a key step in the legal slide to dictatorship.

items.q: When did the systematic mass murder of Jews begin?
items.correct: Mass shootings by Einsatzgruppen began with Operation Barbarossa in June 1941; industrial extermination camps followed from 1942.
items.misconception: The Holocaust began as soon as Hitler came to power in 1933.
items.random_wrong: The killings only began after Germany lost the Battle of Stalingrad.
items.why: Persecution began in 1933, but systematic murder only started in 1941 with the invasion of the USSR. Students conflate persecution, ghettoisation and extermination as a single early policy.

items.q: What were the three Ks for Nazi women?
items.why: Correct answer: Kinder, Küche, Kirche. “Krieg, Kapital, Kunst” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What did Strength through Joy provide?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Cheap holidays and leisure. “Free schooling” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: By what year was Hitler Youth made compulsory?
items.why: Exact answer: 1936. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
